Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I was going to sit down and study, but then I'd just kind of drift through the day and suddenly it would be midnight. When I did study, it was usually reading through notes or course materials, but I couldn't stick to any schedule. I'd maybe get 11 hours in a week if I was lucky, but it was scattered and unfocused.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then panic when I remembered a deadline was coming. Sometimes I'd have a burst of energy and think I'd finally get on track, but it never lasted. Life stuff always seemed to get in the way, or I'd just feel too anxious to start.
Q3: Usually it was fear. I'd get this wave of anxiety thinking about how behind I was, and I'd force myself to log in just to see what I was missing. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about something due and that would push me to check. But honestly, a lot of times I'd log in, feel overwhelmed by everything I hadn't done, and just close the tab.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a couple of weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit to check in every morning, but that fell apart pretty quickly. The LMS never felt comfortable or routine to me—it always felt like opening a door to all my failures. Every time I logged in, I saw how much I was behind.
Q5: The reading materials were the most useful for me since I learn best by reading and writing things out. When I could download a PDF and read through it at my own pace, that worked. The videos were okay too, I watched quite a few hours of them when I could bring myself to focus. But I needed to process things in text form to really understand.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums most of the time. Posting there felt terrifying—like everyone would see how lost I was. I also avoided a lot of the assignments until it was too late. I'd look at them, feel this wave of anxiety about not knowing where to start, and then just... not do them. It's embarrassing how many I left incomplete.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to something I needed to know. If it was clearly explained and didn't require me to interact with other people, I was more likely to engage. Anything that felt ambiguous or open-ended just made my anxiety spike and I'd avoid it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps, honestly. I'd think about doing them, but then I'd remember all the required stuff I hadn't finished and feel guilty. Required activities carried this weight of dread, but at least I knew I had to attempt them eventually. Optional ones just added to my mental list of things I was failing at.
Q9: Yes, actually. When I did manage to study, a lot of it was with downloaded PDFs and handwritten notes. I'd print things out or copy text into my own documents and work through it that way. I also used some YouTube videos to understand concepts the course materials didn't explain well enough for me.
Q10: There were nights where I'd spend hours reading through my printed notes and rewriting key concepts, trying to make sense of everything. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also sometimes just sit with the textbook, highlighting and annotating, completely offline. My best studying probably happened away from the computer entirely.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a lecture PDF or reading, maybe skim it online once, and then work with the printed version for days. The LMS would only show that one quick access, but I might have spent hours with that material afterward. I just couldn't stand reading everything on screen.
Q12: There were several points where I just couldn't bring myself to engage at all. When I fell behind on a few assignments, this spiral started where I felt too scared to even look at the course. The anxiety would build up and I'd avoid everything. The more I avoided, the worse it got, and then I'd feel even more scared to check in.
Q13: Absolutely. I had competing responsibilities that made it hard to focus, and when I was tired or overwhelmed, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Confusion was a big one too—when I didn't understand something, instead of asking for help, I'd just freeze up. My anxiety made everything feel ten times harder than it probably was.
Q14: All the time. I'd log in, click through some videos or readings, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about everything I hadn't done, or worrying about failing, and nothing would actually sink in. I'd watch a whole video and realize I couldn't remember a single thing from it.
Q15: I postponed almost everything important. The bigger and more important an assignment was, the more terrified I felt about starting it. I knew I was sabotaging myself, but the anxiety was paralyzing. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd feel the same fear all over again.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that sometimes got me to act, but usually too late. I'd ignore something until the deadline was right on top of me, then panic and either rush through something inadequate or miss it entirely. The deadline pressure didn't motivate me early—it just made the final hours before incredibly stressful.
Q17: I didn't get much feedback because I didn't submit much work. When I did get feedback on the few assignments I completed, it was usually good, which made me feel worse somehow—like I was capable but just couldn't get myself together. It didn't really change my behavior though; the anxiety was too strong.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming with so many online courses happening at once. Having 14 courses meant I could never feel on top of anything. The course itself was probably fine, but I couldn't keep track of everything, and that made me disengage more. A simpler structure might have helped me stay focused.
Q19: The lack of clear, small steps made things harder. When assignments were big and vague, I'd freeze. If there had been more broken-down tasks with mini-deadlines, maybe I could have managed better. Also, the discussion requirements felt intimidating for someone like me who's scared of being judged by peers.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me—logging in, clicking around, watching videos, trying to understand things, but being too anxious or confused to actually complete and submit work. You can be present in the system without producing anything. Fear of failure can make you engage with materials but never commit to submitting anything.
Q21: That wasn't really my experience since I had low activity and also performed poorly. But I can see how someone who studies effectively offline, with textbooks and notes, might not need the LMS much. If they understand the material and can perform on exams, the LMS activity doesn't matter. I just couldn't pull that off.
Q22: A course log can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but it can't show what was actually happening in your head. It can't capture anxiety, avoidance, or the hours spent studying offline. It misses all the emotional struggle behind the numbers. My low completion rate doesn't show how much I actually wanted to succeed.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about the material and putting in effort to understand it, not just going through the motions. It means feeling connected to what you're learning and being able to focus. For me, I wanted to be engaged, but my anxiety kept pulling me away. True engagement requires mental presence, not just physical clicks.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how scared I was all the time. It wouldn't show the nights I spent worrying about the course instead of sleeping, or the times I cried because I felt so behind. It wouldn't show that I actually cared deeply about doing well—I just couldn't overcome the fear and avoidance. My low numbers look like apathy, but it was really anxiety.
